history: The client has recently experienced the deaths of an uncle and a cousin, which has intensified his anxiety about his parents' health, especially his father's. There is a longstanding pattern of emotional distance and lack of transparency between the client and his father, particularly regarding health issues. The client finds refuge in mathematics, describing it as a structured and controllable world compared to the emotional unpredictability of his family life. He often feels 'behind the scenes' in his own life and struggles with feelings of being 'less than' when unable to understand complex ideas, paralleling his difficulties in family relationships.
intermediate_belief: If I can't understand or control what is happening with my father's health, then I will feel overwhelmed and disconnected. If I avoid emotional situations, I can protect myself from pain and uncertainty. If I focus on things I can master, like math, I can feel competent and safe.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't make sense of my family's problems or connect with my father, then I will always feel lost and behind, and nothing I do will matter. If I avoid these issues, I will just keep feeling empty and alone.
coping_strategies: The client immerses himself in mathematics and teaching as a way to avoid confronting emotional distress related to his family. He avoids direct interaction with his father, especially when thinking about the possibility of his father's death. This avoidance provides temporary relief but maintains his anxiety and emotional distance.
situation: Thinking about the possibility of the father dying before the mother. Avoiding interaction with the father.
auto_thought: If my father dies before my mother, I won't be able to handle it. I can't face him or deal with this uncertainty.
behavior: I avoid talking to or spending time with my father. I distract myself with math or teaching activities instead of engaging with him.
